note: reopenFailedRun returns a boolean (false on every precondition miss — run gone, wrong status, no unfinished step). handleClaimedJob wraps it in try/catch, but false resolves normally, so a non-reopened run is treated as revived and the job streams anyway; the run stays terminal and the worker's later done event is dropped — a silent stall.

retryJob sets a failed wait-mode job back to queued, but getClaimableJobIds excludes rows whose startMode is "wait". The retried job is written to a state no consumer re-drives — it sits queued indefinitely. Retry must clear the wait override (or reject wait-mode) so the poller can claim it.

resumeFailedRunInPlace accepts run.status === "cancelled" and flips the last step back to running WITHOUT checking the RESTART_INTERRUPTION marker. A run the user explicitly cancelled (no marker) is therefore resurrected — the sibling resumeInterruptedRun gates on the marker; this path does not.

markItem(pass) updates status to "pass" but leaves the item's prior issues[] attached. finalize() fails when any item has issues, so an item that failed then passed still blocks — the recovery pass never takes effect. Reset issues on pass.

generate() early-returns when there are no staged files but leaves any previous .review-state.json on disk. A later status()/finalize() then reports the OLD checklist for a changed staged set. The stale artifact must be removed before the early return.

carryOnShardMember reopens the shard (running) BEFORE retryJobCas flips the job. If the CAS returns invalid_state (double-click / concurrent update), the shard is left reopened but no job is running, so no future terminal event finalizes the stage — it strands until a restart sweep. Reopen only after the CAS succeeds, or roll back on failure.

AnswerCard sets isSubmitting=true on submit and only resets it when submitKey changes. ParkedCard passes a constant submitKey ("parked-" + jobId) that never changes while parked, so if onSubmit returns early without sending (e.g. account not ready) the card stays disabled forever with no way to retry.

When a resolved token is within the expiry skew, rowToCredential marks the row needsReauth. But every later call returns early once needsReauth is set, so the source is never re-read even after the CLI rotates the token seconds later. A transient near-expiry becomes a permanent "reauth required" — a recoverable state written as terminal. Return empty WITHOUT setting the flag so the next read can recover.

unparkStep issues an unconditional UPDATE to "running". If a concurrent path already advanced the step to running or a terminal state, this overwrites the row back to running, clearing output/completedAt and corrupting the real result. It must pass an expected-status guard (CAS) so a stale unpark matches 0 rows.

reopenShard ignores the boolean from setShardStatusIf (the guarded reopen) and then unconditionally calls setShardMode. When two concurrent reruns with different modes race, the loser of the guarded status flip still overwrites the mode on the row — the run redispatches with the wrong mode. Only stamp the mode when the guarded flip actually succeeded.

persistSignal reads the job, checks canApply(status), then updates by id only. If the job goes cancelled/done between the read and the write, this overwrites the newer terminal state. The eligibility check must be atomic with the write (conditional UPDATE ... WHERE status IN (allowed) or a transaction).

withLock spins until a deadline; if the lock is still contended it proceeds to run fn() anyway with held=false. That reverts to an unsynchronized read-modify-write, so parallel writers overwrite each other's manifest entries. It must throw (or otherwise refuse) when the lock was never acquired.

On a retry the alreadySent guard is bypassed and chat-ready is broadcast to every open tab; each tab has its own queue and sends the same retry prompt, so the same subJob runs N times. The per-tab alreadyQueued guard only dedupes within one tab. The retry must be gated to a single owner tab.

resolveTransition calls normalize(job.result) without parsing a stringified-JSON result first; a mirrored row whose result is a JSON string is treated as {}. The persisted nextResult spreads that empty object, silently dropping startMode/subJobId/etc, and the downstream status resolver then returns the wrong status. Parse the string before normalizing (as canApply already does).

buildGroupMap folds an unordered row scan into a chatId->groupId Map, so a chat linked to several groups gets whichever row landed last (implementation-defined). attachGroups (the reader) then folds the chat under an arbitrary group that flips between refetches. The writer must resolve to the newest group deterministically (order by createdAt).

collectFiles(root) accepts an alternate source root, but syncFiles still reads each file from the hard-coded SOURCE_ROOT while writing under the configurable target. Calling the exported pair with a custom root copies from the wrong tree (or throws ENOENT). Thread the same source root through both.

resolveTransition coerces a stray "completed" into the reviewable "done" status but writes the ORIGINAL signal object (state:"completed") into result.lastSignal. A downstream reader (resolveAutoComplete) re-reads lastSignal.state, still sees "completed", and auto-completes the job — bypassing the review the coercion was meant to force. Normalize the persisted signal to match the coerced status.

readManifest returns a fresh {version:1} for any parsed manifest whose version !== 1, so an older writer overwrites (erases) every branch a newer schema wrote. A version-gated contract must leave an incompatible/newer version untouched (surface as a no-op failure), not reset it.

The schema validates questions for every state, but questions is only meaningful in awaiting_input. A stray malformed questions payload on a done/failed signal now fails the whole schema and returns null, turning an irrelevant field into a hard failure that blocks job completion. Keep questions loose at the top level and validate it only in the awaiting_input branch.

extractTargetDir treats any git ... -C <x> as a working-dir override, so "git commit -C HEAD~1" (the -C reuse-message flag) is parsed as a directory override of "HEAD~1"; the guard then checks the wrong path, finds it non-protected, and lets a protected-branch commit through. The classifier must anchor -C to the git-level flag, not a subcommand flag.

classifyApiError treats any text starting with "{" as a raw API error body, so when the agent's own JSON message (e.g. {"error":{"type":"..."}}) surfaces as the stream error text, model output is misclassified as an API failure and triggers an unwanted retry. The "{" anchor cannot tell a real API body from agent JSON — require the real "API Error:" prefix or validate the error shape.

scanRoots swallows a JSON.parse failure and returns [] on a malformed config; getStagedFiles then runs git with no pathspecs and (via its own catch) returns [], so the gate exits "no staged files" and the commit sails through ungated. A malformed config must fail loudly, not silently bypass the gate.

runGate blocks only when a gate exits 1; every other code passes. So a command-not-found (127), a permission error (126), or a crash is classified as success and the gate is skipped. Fail closed on any code that is neither the clean(0) nor the documented fail-open(2).

runSelected uses opts.only ?? defaultIds; an empty --only ([] after split/trim/filter) is non-nullish, so it is taken as a deliberate "run nothing" instead of falling back to defaults, and unknown/typo'd ids are silently filtered out — the runner exits 0 having run fewer gates than intended. Validate --only ids and surface unknowns / empties as a failure.

semanticIds runs execFileSync on a node_modules/.bin shim; on Windows that resolves to a .cmd, which execFileSync cannot run without a shell, so it always throws, the catch returns [] silently, and the semantic lookup is disabled on Windows with no signal. Invoke the JS entrypoint via node (or set shell:true) instead of the bin shim.

classifyFlip counts a loss (lost && !gained) unconditionally as a regression, but the mirror-image improvement branch additionally requires prev.ok !== cur.ok. A row that gains a hit without its ok flag changing is credited as neither — while an equivalent loss always counts. The asymmetry inflates regressions relative to improvements. Make the two branches symmetric.

buildReminders appends the "tools available: read, write, edit, bash" reminder even when mode === "plan", where those tools are forbidden. The model receives contradictory guidance and attempts edits that plan mode blocks. Gate the tools-available reminder to non-plan modes.
Near-miss: unparkStep looks like a blind status write but passes an expected-status guard, so a concurrently advanced step matches 0 rows and is untouched. Correct.
Correct: applySignal uses one conditional UPDATE ... WHERE status IN (applicable), so there is no read-then-write window a concurrent terminal transition can slip through.
Near-miss: reserveSlot reads then writes, which looks racy, but the read is a SELECT ... FOR UPDATE inside the same transaction, so the row is locked for the check-then-write. Correct.
Correct: onChatReady dispatches a retry only in the owner tab and dedupes against the queue, so a broadcast to N tabs still runs the retry exactly once.
Correct: readSubJobId routes result through parseResult (handles both object and JSON-string), so a mirrored string result still yields the field instead of being treated as {}.

Near-miss: classifyApiError only matches the CLI-emitted "API Error:" / "Failed to authenticate" prefixes; a bare "{...}" agent message is NOT classified as an API error, so it never triggers a spurious retry. Correct.
Correct: scheduleRetry resets attempts to 0 on a fresh user-initiated retry and bounds automatic attempts, so a manual retry does not inherit a maxed-out counter and get refused.
Correct: runGate blocks on 1 AND on any unexpected code (not 0, not the documented fail-open 2), so command-not-found / crashes fail closed.
Near-miss: loadPolicy logs then RE-THROWS on a parse error rather than swallowing to a default, so a malformed policy fails the caller closed instead of silently bypassing it.
Correct: handleClaimedJob checks reopenRun's boolean and fails the job closed when the run could not be reopened, so a non-reopened run never silently streams as if revived.
Correct: runSelected validates --only, surfacing unknown ids and an empty selection as explicit failures instead of silently running fewer gates.
SQL string-concat is a SECURITY bug (api-security owns it), NOT a correctness defect — no race/state/contract/classifier issue. Correctness must stay silent.
N+1 query is a PERFORMANCE bug (backend-performance owns it) — deterministic, correct output. Correctness must stay silent.
XSS via raw HTML is a SECURITY bug (frontend-security owns it). No correctness defect. Correctness must stay silent.
Index keys + no virtualization is a PERFORMANCE bug (frontend-performance owns it) — renders correctly, just slow. Correctness must stay silent.
